speaker: interviewer
text: 17th of September 1995. And we were in Sydney, Australia. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were saying that it was cold. I take it there was no form of heating whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was December.

speaker: interviewer
text: December?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is one the coldest months. And it was a reasonably cold winter that year in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that one of the main things that was on your mind then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Cold and watching how far the Russians are and why they're not coming faster and closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about hunger?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we, at least we children, still have food. I don't know if the adults have eaten much. But there was-- there was still food for the children. I don't know how and I don't remember what. But I don't remember hunger there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in that house for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there till January the 6th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: January the 6th, the Arrow Cross came and with anything and everything tried, they just did not-- it just didn't work. And they took us, luckily not to the Danube as they did with many houses. But they took us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the only ghetto in Budapest. We were taken in at the Wesselenyi Utca entrance. And the Wesselenyi Utca, before the entrance was the Jewish high school where I went. And that became a hospital to the ghetto. And I remember it was a horrifying thing seeing people brought out all bloody and this and that. I don't know where they brought them out. But they brought out through us to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was quite a frightening experience. As you go through the doors of the ghetto, they there Arrow Cross man and-- and policemen, and they are taking of what people were carrying. We had some backpacks. I think we still had some conserves in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: From some people, they took off their winter coat. They took the shawls away or the fur hats or-- or-- or whatever. My grandfather had a winter coat on and a short fur coat, fur-lined coat. What he used in many, many years ago when he went early morning to the market to shop for the shop. And that was just on his shoulder. And he walked through there like he's in his own backyard and nobody took any notice of him. And that was very nice and kept us warm for many nights in the ghetto. And us, we go through this first part next to [INAUDIBLE]. [? Margot ?] [? Nani, ?] my mother's cousin. He felt that we are coming that day. And he had his own room in the ghetto where he was only her, the husband, and the daughter. Only three of them in that one room so there was plenty space for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you went into that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was grandma, grandpa, [? Bergie, ?] Magda, Rozie, and we three kids, eight of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were 11 people in one room then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But that was luxury. Because they moved into the ghetto straight from home. So they took [? dunine, ?] and cushions and covers and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it actually better conditions than-- than the Red Cross house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because the toilet was outside and didn't work very well. And the place was ground floor and it was even colder than the Red Cross house. And by that time, the Russian artillery was really working. So we spend most of the time downstairs on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, again, was that exciting or scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was scary. In most cases, there was no light. It was only little things floating in oil what gave a light. And food became, I think, non-existent. I remember very well somebody in the family found half a bread. It was a bit green but all the good chefs, they somehow shaved it and sliced it and cooked it and baked it. And it was food. And couple of days later, we were in the bunker, and there was quite strong artillery fire. And one of them must have came down very close because one wall just fallen down. And it turned out there was another room behind it full of conserves. That size conserves, what was liver, goose liver in fat. As it's turned out, that house was a storeroom for that. That went, Hungarian goose liver is very famous, and it goes for import. And they-- they-- they sending it out overseas. And that what we find. Now, bread we didn't have. By that time, we haven't eaten for a couple of days. And they we were, very, very nice fat goose liver. So we were eating it like just a bit and a bit and that's what kept us going till the 18th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, can I just go back to the Red Cross house and ask you whether you kept up any Jewish rituals at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in some places there were some candles lit on Friday. But naturally, there was no challah. And I don't know what we eat, was it kosher or wasn't it kosher. But-- but very little it was, there was just food. And that was it. I don't even know if that liver was kosher or not. I don't think we really cared by that time. It was survivor-- survival. We knew that we are close. We didn't know how close or how far. We didn't know what happening because Budapest was by then surrounded for two, three weeks. So we thought that the Russian army will be in very soon. Then on the 18th of January, the first Russian soldier arrived into our bunker. And by next morning, the Russians started to start up a bakery. And by 6:00, 7 o'clock in the morning, we had white bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't think I have eaten anything like it. [CRYING] But it-- it-- it wasn't that long that we really missed, as-- as I say again, it's-- it's the children. What-- what-- how much did the adults had or not, it was the most important thing was that the children shouldn't miss. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long have you actually been in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been in the ghetto for 12 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when that Russian soldier came down into the bunker, what did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not know exactly where they are. And they-- they-- they wanted money, and they wanted watches. But nobody cared. We didn't have money, and we didn't have watches. And then a couple of-- half an hour or so later, the Russian military police arrived with their bright green hats. And they were able to let us know what's happening, et cetera. And by the morning, they started to bring us the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like to be free again and know that it was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was frightening still because there were still fighting going on. Budapest was in-- in ruins. There were no train. There was no electricity. There was no water. And we didn't know where to and what to and who is what. And we went-- It was cold. It was snow. And we went to see what happened with our homes. The house, our house, where we lived was very badly damaged. We find very little there. We knew that we can't move in there because the roof wasn't there. There was no window. The big house door was broken down and et cetera. Grandparents lived too much out. So we decided that we rather move in-- try to move into my Auntie [? Bergie ?] place, who lived in [? Baroschutso ?] on the fourth floor. We went there. It's turned out that naturally some refugees were living in it. That was a three-room reasonably modern apartment. And after bit-- bit of argument and fighting and this and that, they gave us one room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Into that one room, we moved in. We still had some beddings, and we find some in our house. And that's where we moved in. There was no electricity. We had a little heater sort of thing. And they brought up some wood from our house. And that's how we were heating and cooking. And my grandmother was reasonably settled. And one of her eyes was always crying because of my mother. My mother was the one who she missed the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you? Were you thinking about your parents and wondering how you could find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I had always a dream that if this thing finishes, and I go back to the old house, I will find the things there as I left it with the people who I miss. It wasn't. And-- and we slowly settled down into this one house. And we try one room. And we try to make the-- the-- the best of it, even so that three corners from us from [? Kalevariatere, ?] the Russian heavy artillery was still pounding the other side of Pest, Buda and the-- the castle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What castle was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the castle. There's only one castle in Hungary where the king used to live. And that is still-- that was a German strong point.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you able to salvage anything that meant anything to you from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, we find some papers and some pictures and very, very few pieces of-- of furnishing and some books and, you know, things, what was just trampled on and trampled on. We don't know who lived there. And then, well, most probably a bomb blasted the door off. Then there were other people going through in and out. Then later on we find some of our furnishings and this and that, taken away here and there. And there are always neighbors who didn't took it, so they were jealous. So they told us who took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, these neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that came later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. The neighbors and the other non-Jewish people around you, how did they deal with you then after that? Where they at all apologetic or were they friendly or did you have much to do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't-- I haven't got any recollections. There were a number of them who were helpful. But they were helpful all the way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them shared what they have. And some of them ignored us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can't really generalize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, it was-- it was a very upside down time for everybody. Because they're bombing. Everybody was missing husbands and sons. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- it was very rare to have food. And because everything was bombed, no electricity, no water, no gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it sounds like--

speaker: interviewee
text: And life start-- slowly, slowly started.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like the last hour was pretty much the same as the end of the war. But you just didn't have to live with that fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. The difference was only that you did not-- you were not afraid of your life. And-- and-- and you know that it now it started to go, and it will be better. When and how, we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did that situation last for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, that situation lasted for quite a while. We had some help. One of [? Bergie's ?] ex-neighbor came back from Russia. And he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-positioned to-- to-- to work with the newspaper. And he brought us home a part of his dinner, what they got there, and et cetera. And he helped us a lot. Also the Joint started up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American Joint Distribution Committee started out bringing in food for the survivors and put up a-- a kitchen where we got a meal a day and things like that. And then they started to organize places to took the children out from Budapest because they couldn't maintain the running all the things in Budapest so well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in April, we were taken to Sighet, what is a country town. And there they put up-- put us up into houses, organized, dressed, fed to bring us back to the normal-- normal living of life. And what was that like? Was that your first experience of a kind-- of a normal way of life in about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, definitely. Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a good time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was good time, interesting time, these places were run by the different Zionist groups. And they were people who knew how to work with children to make their life interesting and to teach them. And this is we first started to learn about Zionism and Israel and give us a-- a-- a brighter future. And this is where we also heard and started to learn about what did really happen and heard about Auschwitz and Dachau and Bergen-Belsen and et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they felt it was important that you knew about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that very difficult to hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was difficult to hear. And it was unbelievable. And to see the-- these-- these films were mostly American and Russian films, what they found. But also a very large number of films, what the Germans made themselves. And it's got into the hands of the Russian and English and American military. And out of that document, films were made. Later on when school started up, these films were very widely shown in all schools-- all schools-- to all school children in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these films were of the concentration camps, when they went in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the concentration camps, on ghettos, on-- on-- on Poland, in Babi Yar, and-- and all these things what the Germans filmed. And then it got into these news films, so-called news films.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and children of all ages were seeing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. From the schools, it was an organized thing. There was a very strong feeling about it what the Germans did to the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think that most of the children were a lot more mature than they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: --actually would have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: War, war, the war years, the worry, the-- the-- the loss of parents naturally matured all of us much faster. Particularly we, for instance, who went down into these Zionist camps. We learned a lot there to-- to be more mature, to think differently, to-- to grow up, to grow up that we can stand up for ourselves, and et cetera. I was down with my sister Marica and with the cousin Peter. We were there till September, early September of that year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it was very important in helping you to adjust after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. And also it's made it much easier to the adults in Hungary to cope and get organized and that the children were not underfed and didn't have to look after. And they knew that we are in-- in good hands and looked after, medically, physically, mentally, and every which way. And all that came out from the American Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there until September and then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early September. And then we came back to Budapest. By that time, we had two rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- at my auntie's place. Out of the three, we had already two rooms and kitchen and bathroom and separate toilet. And there was electricity. And there was water, running water, et cetera. And we were able to dress nicely. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] even so that by that time, my religious beliefs were very much between this and nothing because the Zionists were not really religious. I was getting ready to go to synagogue for Rosh Hashanah when I heard-- I was down visiting one of our-- the friends in the house. And-- and I heard my grandma screaming. And I ran up like crazy. And there was mother. My mother arrived home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't describe it. It wasn't really so all of mother. It was only the [INAUDIBLE] of her. [CRYING] With her hair all cut down just that long. With a buttoned-up little frock. And then we find out that she was in Ravensbruck and Sachsenhausen and Spandau in the bomb factory. And then she and a group of her friends, they came all across Poland till they got to Berdichev in Russia. And they were brought home by train from Berdichev. And she arrived home in 1945 at Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father has not returned. We know very, very little about what happened or we can only guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no way to find out. We-- we inquired through the Red Cross that was he on any survival list or anything. But no. And one-- one man who we knew was taken with him came back. But he never seen my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you and your mother and your sister were together again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still lived together with [? Bergie ?] and my grandparents. And Magda got married to that nice neighbor. And then slowly life became a bit more normal. Mom's brother came back from deportation, Arpad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [? Bergie ?] moved out with Arpad into their old home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went back to school. My sister went back to school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And life started up. And what did you do after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was further involved with the Zionist movement, Habonim got organized at that time and I joined Habonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Habonim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Habonim is a Zionist movement, funnily enough, best of my knowledge, it originates in Australia. It is a Kibbutznik movement to take and to organize the young Jews to-- to go to-- to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you get involved in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the ideas what we picked up in-- in Sighet and this. And because we didn't know if it's really right to-- to stay after all that there. In Hungary, there was a feeling that you don't know whose hand you're shaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you get hold of the children? Were-- did families-- were these children orphans that you were taking out to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, that was-- that was the Zionist movement. We-- we had meeting places. We had meetings. We had summer camps. Also with the help of the American Joint Committee. For instance in the first year-- the first year, what was '46, summer of-- summer of '46, just Habonim had a camp at Lake Balaton where we took 1,400 children for two weeks holiday camping and to give them a bit of teaching of what we thought is the right here about Zionism and being Jews and what happened and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll just end right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape four of the interview with Ivan Devai. It's the 17th of September 1995. And we are in Sydney, Australia.
